skill.name: dotnet-backend-patterns
skill.description: Master C#/.NET backend development patterns for building robust APIs, MCP servers, and enterprise applications. Covers async/await, dependency injection, Entity Framework Core, Dapper, configuration, and production best practices.
skill.summary: Production-ready C#/.NET patterns for APIs, microservices, and enterprise backends with EF Core, Dapper, and clean architecture.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 134 static analysis findings are false positives. The 'backtick execution' patterns (87 locations) are markdown code fences in documentation files. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings (11 locations) are LINQ Expression<Func> patterns for the Specification pattern. The 'system reconnaissance' and 'filesystem' findings are normal C# property access and YAML frontmatter. This is a legitimate educational skill containing documentation and code examples for .NET backend development.

content.user_title: Build production .NET backends with modern patterns
content.value_statement: Developing enterprise .NET applications requires mastering complex patterns for data access, dependency injection, and clean architecture. This skill provides battle-tested patterns for EF Core, Dapper, caching, and resilient API design.
actual_capabilities: Generate repository and service layer templates following clean architecture
Optimize EF Core queries with AsNoTracking, Include, and compiled queries
Implement Dapper patterns for high-performance data access
Configure dependency injection with proper lifetime management
Design caching strategies using Redis and memory cache
Apply specification pattern for complex query composition
limitations: Focused on .NET/C# projects only - not applicable to other languages
Provides backend patterns only - does not cover frontend or UI development
Code examples assume .NET 6+ and modern C# features
Does not replace hands-on testing or code review processes

use_cases.title: Greenfield API Development
use_cases.description: Build a new REST API from scratch with proper layering, dependency injection, and data access patterns.

use_cases.title: Legacy Code Modernization
use_cases.description: Refactor existing .NET Framework code to modern .NET patterns with improved performance and maintainability.
use_cases.target_user: Technical leads managing legacy system migration

use_cases.title: Performance Optimization Review
use_cases.description: Review and optimize existing data access code for EF Core or Dapper-based applications.

prompt_templates.title: Repository Pattern Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a repository pattern implementation for a Product entity with CRUD operations using Entity Framework Core. Include async methods, proper disposal, and follow clean architecture principles.

prompt_templates.title: Query Optimization Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this EF Core query for performance issues. Identify N+1 problems, missing AsNoTracking calls, and opportunities for projection. Suggest optimized alternatives.
prompt_templates.scenario: Code review scenario with existing inefficient query

prompt_templates.title: Dapper Micro-ORM Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a Dapper-based repository for high-performance read operations. Include connection management, parameter handling, and multi-mapping for joined tables.
prompt_templates.scenario: High-traffic read endpoint requiring maximum performance

prompt_templates.title: Clean Architecture Service Layer
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a service layer that orchestrates repository calls, caching, and validation. Apply dependency injection, CQRS separation, and resilience patterns for external calls.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building application layer in clean architecture solution

output_examples.input: Generate a repository pattern for Order entity
output_examples.output: A complete OrderRepository class with async CRUD methods, using IDbConnectionFactory for connection management, proper CancellationToken support, and Dapper for query execution

output_examples.input: Optimize this EF Core query with multiple includes
output_examples.output: Refactored query using AsNoTracking, AsSplitQuery for large collections, Select projection to DTO, and CompiledAsyncQuery for frequently executed queries

best_practices: Use AsNoTracking for read-only queries to reduce memory overhead
Register database contexts and connections as Scoped lifetime in DI
Apply the specification pattern for complex, composable query criteria
anti_patterns: Synchronous database calls in async code paths - always use Async methods
Loading entire entities when only specific columns are needed
Allowing lazy loading to trigger N+1 query problems

faq.question: When should I use Dapper instead of EF Core?
faq.answer: Use Dapper for performance-critical queries, complex SQL (CTEs, window functions), or read-heavy workloads. Use EF Core for rich domain models with relationships, change tracking needs, or when you prefer LINQ-to-SQL translation.

faq.question: What is the specification pattern and when should I use it?
faq.answer: The specification pattern encapsulates query criteria in reusable classes. Use it for complex queries that are reused across your application, or when you need composable filter criteria with paging and sorting.

faq.question: How do I prevent N+1 query problems in EF Core?
faq.answer: Use Include for eager loading related data, avoid lazy loading in loops, and use AsSplitQuery when including multiple collections to prevent cartesian explosion.

faq.question: What is the recommended service lifetime for DbContext?
faq.answer: Scoped lifetime is recommended for DbContext in web applications. This ensures one instance per HTTP request, which is required for change tracking and identity map patterns.

faq.question: How can I improve EF Core query performance?
faq.answer: Use AsNoTracking for read-only queries, project to DTOs with Select, use compiled queries for hot paths, apply proper indexing, and avoid client-side evaluation by ensuring queries can translate to SQL.

faq.question: What caching strategy should I implement?
faq.answer: Use memory caching for small, frequently accessed data within a single instance. Use Redis for distributed caching across multiple instances. Always set expiration times and have cache miss fallback logic.
